div.p: Alexander, by the grace of God king of the Scots, to all worthy men of the whole of his land, clerk as well as lay, sends greeting. May you all know that in our presence and of our barons at the colloquium at Liston in the Year of Grace 1235 an amicable agreement was made between the Abbot and Monks of Melrose on the one side, and Roger Avenel on the other, over the disputes instigated between them, that is, how Roger, contrary to the tenor of his charter and those of his predecessors, put a horse-stud and his own animals and men into the Monks’ land in Eskdale within the boundaries set out in his charter, and how the said Roger and his men destroyed some houses built by the said monks, and levelled their dykes and other enclosures of theirs, and brought other injuries upon them, as they said. To wit that Roger has allowed that neither he himself nor his heirs nor their men shall have a horse-stud, cattle, pigs, sheep or any other live-stock within the boundaries specified in the said Roger’s charter, unless by the monks’ permission, saving to the said Roger the beasts of the chase in the manner stated in his charter, which we and our barons have explained in this way; to wit, that Roger and his heirs shall have so many stag and hind, boar and sow, and roe-bucks and roe-does; in addition, the eyries of hawks and sparrow-hawks, provided that the monks do not maliciously cause hindrance so that they can nest in the place where they nest as long as they have the habit of nesting there; they should not remove the nests of hawks or sparrow-hawks within the said boundaries, nor should they fell the trees in which they nest in one year until it is determined whether they wish to nest in those trees in the following year or not; and if then they have not nested, let the monks make use of the trees by all means that they wish. In other places within the boundaries set out in the charter of the said Roger, they may build lodges and buildings wherever they wish, according to their pleasure and intent, and make use of the tenement contained within the said boundaries as shall seem to them best and most usefully profitable to themselves, their brethren, servants, and livestock. In addition, thus have we determined about thieves and trespassers: that they are not deemed trespassers in respect of the right of the monks and the use of the same, of their tenants, brethren, and men, whenever they go for grazing their own animals, or keeping watch, or searching, or carrying out their other business, whether by day or by night, unless they have been caught in clear wrongdoing. And if they have been discovered in such a way at night, they should be guarded until the next day; and if they are tenants of the monks, they should straightaway freely be given up in order to obey the law where they ought to obey. When captured by day, they should be returned immediately to them. Also the said monks shall not hunt there with packs of hounds and ropes, nor lead others to that place for hunting, nor set traps there except for catching wolves. Accordingly the said Roger and his heirs shall enjoy the wild game as was agreed before, without deceit and bad intention, without loss and injury of crops, hedges, meadows, livestock, animals and all the other possessions of the same monks. As witnesses, Andrew of Moray, and Clement of Dunblane, bishops; Walter, earl of Menteith; Walter son of Alan, steward of Scotland; Walter Oliphant, justiciar of Lothian; Henry de Balliol; David the Marischal; Geoffrey the clerk of the Liverance. On the eighth day of April in the twenty-second year of the reign of our lord the king [1236].
